problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research / "Huskies" soccer team coordination). The team is given event-stream passing and duel data for a fictional team's 38-game season and asked to analyze player interactions, team structure, strategy/playing style, and to recommend how the Huskies can improve, then generalize teamwork lessons to other team types. This O-award paper ("Rock, Paper, Scissors, Shoot!") models games as directed weighted passing networks, mines network metrics correlated with shot count, classifies each team-half into one of four real football playing styles, and builds a strategy-vs-counterstrategy (game-theoretic) recommendation table.
models_used: Directed weighted passing networks (digraphs) built at three time scales: whole game, game half, and rolling n-pass subnetworks (n = 50, 100), with players as nodes and pass-count edge weights P_{i,j}

Dyadic/triadic configuration model: custom Link L_{i,j}=P_{i,j}, Reciprocal Link R_{i,j}=P_{i,j}+P_{j,i}, and Triad T_{i,j,k}=R_{i,j}+R_{i,k}+R_{j,k} strength scores to quantify player chemistry (position/location-specific, as a deliberate alternative to Gursakal motifs)

Network science metric battery: passing-network centroid (x,y), player dispersion C around centroid, advance ratio, weighted local/mean clustering coefficient, closeness centrality (via Dijkstra shortest paths on inverse-pass-weight edges), largest eigenvalue λ1 of weighted adjacency matrix, betweenness centrality, network centralization ω, algebraic connectivity λ2 (2nd-smallest Laplacian eigenvalue)

Three original possession-change metrics: Offensive Duel Centroid, Defensive Duel Centroid (mean x-position of contested duels on each half of field), and Average Press Time t_press (time between gaining possession and opponent initiating a duel)

Multiple linear regression of metric feature vectors onto shot count, evaluated separately for n-pass networks, halves, and full games (best model R^2 = 0.4668 at half scale); used both for prediction and for p-value-based feature ranking/selection (top 6 metrics kept)

Playing-style classification: four real styles (Tiki-Taka, Long Ball, High Pressing, Park-the-Bus) defined as centroids in 6-D metric space (coordinates set at integer multiples of ±1 or ±2 standard deviations from league mean); each team-half assigned to nearest style by 6-D Euclidean distance d_{i,S}

Rock-Paper-Scissors game-theoretic model: empirical win/draw/loss frequencies tabulated for every (own style x opponent style) pairing, reduced to a decision table that prescribes the style maximizing win prob, maximizing draw prob, or minimizing loss prob given the opponent's observed style
key_techniques: Choosing shot count (not goals) as the dependent variable, justified quantitatively with Premier League data (7.2% of goals from set/penalty kicks; Crystal Palace 17.8% from penalties) to argue goals are contaminated by individual skill/luck and goalkeeping

Inventing possession-change metrics (duel centroids, press time) to fill a gap the team explicitly identified in the literature, then proving their worth — all three landed among the highest-ranked features by correlation with shots

Multi-time-scale modeling (n-pass / half / full game) to separate micro-coordination from macro-strategy, with the team reporting that fine scales (R^2 0.01-0.03) failed and the half scale (R^2 0.47) worked
Honest negative results: the dyad/triad chemistry model is reported as a failure (R^2 = 0.0298 / 0.0137) and used as narrative motivation to pivot to the next model

p-value/R^2 feature ranking table (Figure 2) to prune ~18 candidate metrics down to the 6 most predictive, with documented manual overrides (kept defensive duel centroid over algebraic connectivity for style-discrimination reasons)
Operationalizing qualitative football styles into quantitative centroids via standard-deviation offsets, then nearest-centroid (minimum Euclidean distance) classification
Reframing 'which metric wins?' as 'which style beats which style?' — turning a weak regression result into a strong game-theoretic insight (the central narrative pivot of the paper)

Sensitivity analysis on the most subjective modeling choice (the SD-scaling of style centroids), showing 1.5x vs 2x vs double scaling reshuffles classifications, and naming Entropy Weighting Method as the principled fix they'd adopt with more time
why_it_won: Judges reward a clear intellectual arc, and this paper has an unusually strong one: it tries the obvious idea (player-chemistry motifs), honestly reports it failing, tries a metric-regression model, reports it as only moderately predictive, and uses both 'failures' to motivate the genuinely original Rock-Paper-Scissors strategy-vs-counterstrategy model — which delivers actionable, intuitive, quantified coaching advice (e.g. play Tiki-Taka vs High Pressing for 64.7% win, play Long Ball vs Tiki-Taka to cap loss at 33.3%). The team demonstrably read the OR/network-science literature (Buldu, Cintia, Clemente, Gursakal) and then went beyond it by inventing possession-change metrics that out-ranked many textbook metrics, directly addressing a gap they articulated. The work is rigorously self-critical (explicit strengths/weaknesses, quantified sensitivity of style centroids, error analysis acknowledging the tiny per-matchup sample), the deliverables map exactly onto the prompt (analysis, recommendation, generalization, coach letter), and the writing is confident and personable (the 'further research is needed... just kidding' joke, the rondo training recommendation citing Guardiola). It earns trust by never overselling weak numbers.
section_highlights: Strong structure throughout. The executive summary on page 1 narrates the full model evolution (motifs failed -> metrics partial -> styles + RPS) so a judge knows the whole story in one read. Model Development (sec 4) is layered cleanly: network construction (4.0.1) -> chemistry configs (4.1) -> full metric catalog with formulas and a notation table (Table 1) -> regression analysis at each scale (4.2) -> feature pruning (4.3) -> style classification and the RPS model (4.4). The dedicated Sensitivity Analysis (sec 5) and Error Analysis (sec 6) sections probe the model's most fragile assumption (SD-scaling of style centroids, Figure 4) and quantify how a single match result swings a win-rate by 11.9%. A Generalization section (sec 7) maps each model component to non-soccer teams (research groups, government, math-modeling teams) and a Future Considerations section lists concrete next steps (hot-zone field-partition networks, nonlinear models, full player positions). The Letter to the Coach (sec 10) translates everything into plain, specific advice (play midfielder 3, drill rondos, use the counter-strategy Table 9). Tables 6-8 are the analytical payoff: per-style win/draw/loss rates and the IF-opponent-style THEN-play-this decision table.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Severe small-sample problem in the headline RPS model: win/draw/loss cells (Table 7) are computed from as few as 1-16 games per style pairing (e.g. Long-Ball vs Park-the-Bus has denominator 2), so a single extra result shifts a win rate by up to 11.9% — the central conclusions rest on thin counts

Playing-style centroids are defined by hand-picked integer multiples of standard deviations (±1, ±2) chosen by team discussion, not data; the paper admits and shows (Figure 4) classifications are highly sensitive to this scaling and that double-scaling collapses almost everything into Park-the-Bus
Only simple/multiple linear regression used; the paper itself flags that nonlinear relationships likely exist and were never modeled, capping explanatory power at R^2≈0.47

Best regression still leaves >53% of shot-count variance unexplained, and most individual metrics have very low standalone R^2 (0.0025-0.31); the model predicts shots within 1.5 only 51.3% of the time
The dyad/triad chemistry model produced essentially no signal (R^2 0.01-0.03); the team suspects the effect exists but their formulation couldn't capture it — an unresolved loose end
On-ball-events-only assumption: no off-ball player positions, so style identification ignores defensive shape of players not touching the ball
Style classification is winner-take-all nearest-centroid (hard assignment), discarding the mixed/ambiguous styles a team actually plays within a half
Generalization to non-soccer teams is largely qualitative and acknowledged to break down for goal-less, multi-objective teams (leadership, preparation, specialists-vs-generalists are unmodeled)
reusable_patterns: The 'failed model as narrative scaffold' pattern: present a reasonable first idea, report it honestly failing with the actual R^2, and use the failure to motivate the next, better model — judges read this as scientific maturity rather than weakness

Pick a defensible proxy for the messy true objective and justify it with external data: here, shot count instead of goals, backed by Premier-League set-piece/penalty statistics — isolates the team-process signal from individual-skill/luck noise

Invent a small number of domain-motivated metrics that target a gap in the literature (possession changes -> duel centroids + press time), then prove their value with the same ranking analysis you apply to textbook metrics
Use a p-value/R^2 ranking table to systematically prune a large candidate-feature set to a small interpretable subset, and document any manual overrides with reasons

Recast a weak predictive result as a strong relational one: when 'no single feature predicts success' falls out, pivot to 'what beats what' — a game-theoretic strategy-vs-counterstrategy table that is both intuitive and directly actionable

Operationalize qualitative categories as centroids in metric space (±k standard deviations) and classify by minimum Euclidean distance — a lightweight, explainable alternative to clustering when you want named, interpretable classes
Multi-time-scale analysis (sub-event windows / half / whole game) to disentangle micro vs macro phenomena and report where signal actually lives

Run sensitivity analysis specifically on your single most subjective modeling choice, quantify how outcomes move, and name the principled method (e.g. Entropy Weighting Method) you'd use to remove the subjectivity — pre-empts the judge's strongest objection
Close with a plain-language stakeholder deliverable (coach letter) that converts every model output into one concrete, named action (play this player, run this drill, use this table)
